speaker: interviewee
text: They got Lillian, and then they made a little infirmary, like, for us. They-- the doctors came-- the [? other ?] Russians, they had no doctors. They had no field doctors. They-- they arrived, and made beds for us. And they gave us only fluid, and we had diarrhea, you know. We had dysentery-- unbelievable. And we couldn't walk from here until-- until the next door, you know. So they tried to give us fluids and things, but they were cooking for the soldiers, too. And it smelled so good, and we were so hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I climbed out of the window and took food, you know. And the doctors didn't understand why the diarrhea didn't want to go away. And they find food under-- under the bed. And they said, 'What you doing? You eat? You want to die yet?' You know, and 'You came out of it, and you want to die?' So they made us understand, 'Don't eat food-- only what we give you.'

speaker: interviewee
text: But Lillian was quite sick, you know. I was not that sick. So I could go to Sweden at that time, you know, after-- to-- to heal up. But Lillian was so close to me, I could not leave her. You know, she had to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went together, and then we-- they took us from the-- the ambulances. They took us from one-- we wanted to go back to Holland. But when I left Holland, my older sister with her baby were still there, and my other sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know my mother and my father were with me. My brother was with me. So I didn't know who was there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was left?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Lillian's-- we wanted to know who was-- we wanted to go back to Holland in the worst way. So we went from one ambulance to another hospital, and they gave us a little bit of food. [INAUDIBLE] and we slowly-- we went slowly closer and closer to Holland. Then we came in a site in Holland, and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get to Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Takes about-- yeah, see, maybe four, five, six days, maybe. I came home-- when I came to Holland, it was the 4th of May. It was even before the whole armistice was there. It was a little bit earlier. But it was-- well, the war-- it was over, but had not signed. It was just in the end of the 5th of May. So it must be end-- end of April. So when we were in-- in the ambulance, this is slowly, we came to-- toward the south of Holland, a little bit part of Holland, in a very, like-- it was a big monastery.

speaker: interviewer
text: A big--

speaker: interviewee
text: A monastery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Monastery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Monastery. We came in the monastery. And it was all the-- all the brothers were staying outside. They had-- it  was like the Capuchin-- they had those brown, brown [INAUDIBLE] with those-- like, those hoods. They stayed all in a line for us. First of all, they have to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like monks? They were monks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Monks, yeah. First of all, I have to tell you something else. We were not the only ones who were in the ambulance. There were other people who were also very sick, came out of concentration camps. They all came out of other camps like [? Gliwice, ?] or [? Birkenau, ?] or Auschwitz. We were [INAUDIBLE] with six, seven people in the ambulance. So when we came in, and the other people [INAUDIBLE] out earlier. There were Dutch people who came in the south of Holland. And then we-- and those people, they bowed for us, you know. They took the-- the hats off and let us go between them. And at the end of the-- of the room was an organ. And they played the Dutch anthem. And suddenly, it came to me-- I'm free. I'm free! I don't believe it. I'm out! [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The first time-- What did you feel? What came over you?

speaker: interviewee
text: An absolutely unbelievable feeling. I made it! I'm not supposed to be here. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewee
text: I then told Lillian, 'You know what? Can you believe it? We're out.'

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were [INAUDIBLE] embraced each other, and we were happy that we had-- we made it. So slowly but surely, but also, we went closer to Amsterdam. And we went to a big, big [? wood, ?] like a big estate. And one of the guys who were also in our-- in our-- in the ambulance, he-- he climbed out, and the door had opened. And the people came out, and he saw suddenly the ambulance coming out. And the doc had bounded, bounded to the guy. The guy was skinny like-- like Lillian-- he was, absolutely. And he was one of the sons from one of the guys. The doc remembered it before the people saw him. And they came over and embraced [INAUDIBLE] own enormous feeling. They gave us coffee, gave us bread. They told us in Amsterdam that people are very hungry. They had no bread. They [INAUDIBLE] people [INAUDIBLE] very, very hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we saved-- so all the bread we could take, we took. And we saved all the bread [INAUDIBLE] hard like a stone. And we-- and we took it to Amsterdam for people who needed it. So we ate what we could-- Did you go back to your home, your original home?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came back in Amsterdam, there was no-- there was no-- and [INAUDIBLE] we were so early, we were not back by bus, not by train, or-- or the trains didn't go. So we went very fast.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we-- there was no committee to-- to pick us up. There was nothing. There we stayed in the middle of Amsterdam. They didn't know where to go. So we-- there was the Salvation Army. And the bus-- the driver from the truck, he went in and said, 'I have here two people from Auschwitz, and they have to find out if their people are there.' We [? can ?] do it. We have to go back. And so we slept in-- in the-- in the Salvation Army. And I said to her-- they asked me, 'Do you know people who live around there?' And so-- and I said, 'Yeah, my sister's-- my sister's brother-in-law-- they-- they-- he's Gentile. He had an antique store in certain street that I remember.' And we went over there. And suddenly, we heard yelling-- you know, [? 'Bepi, ?] Bepi, Bepi, are you back? Are you back?' So that was my sister-in-law-- not my sisters-- my sister's family which had-- her sister-in-law. They came bounding on, and [? now ?] we are very close together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I hear that my-- that my second sister was still alive-- at the hospital, still alive, that her baby was still alive. They took her-- my sister [? Freda, ?] when she was in hiding, she was pregnant. And when she was [INAUDIBLE] the baby, it was three days old. They took the baby away, and they was by-- hiding by-- he was an opera singer, [? Teo ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And he-- they took the child. And my sister didn't know where he was, or if it was good or bad. So after three years, and the war was over, she found out that she had-- that the baby was still--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: That she had the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had the baby. She knew she had the baby, but she didn't know where she was.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then she found out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then she had to say to the people that--

speaker: interviewer
text: She left with him with.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they had to give him back. But those people were so fabulous. You know, they came everyday to my sister and my brother-and-law with the child and slowly let him--

speaker: interviewer
text: But he didn't know who was the mother then. My sister, for sure, was not the mother, you know? So slowly, they started to-- to-- still to the day from today, he still calls his stepfather 'Father.' And his-- his father died, but-- When did your husband return?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came back-- we came back, and my sister was there. My sister took us in. We were full with sores. We were full with-- we-- oh, couldn't-- we could not. And we were still from the-- from the experiments, we started to bleed many times, and blisters. It was not healing up very easily.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were in-- in her house. We were sleeping in a separate room. And suddenly, my brother came from Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very lucky. My brother came from Russia. They-- they thought he was dead, but he-- he came back. And then about 10 days later, my husband came back. But he was an-- I told you he was an artist. He made paintings for the-- for the soldiers, for-- you know, he'd get some extra bread. And he was also sick. He was mentally not very well. So he came back. And then when everything was actually supposed to be very well, I got very, very sick. I was so mixed up, I thought the day was the night, and my father and mother were alive. And I-- I saw people without faces, and I was absolutely a mess.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was over three and  a half months in a mental hospital. They took me in. And and they said-- well, it was too much. It was too much. So I was-- I didn't want to leave. And so [INAUDIBLE] my-- my girlfriend [INAUDIBLE] in Auschwitz with me, and she came back. And she hanged herself. I came to her house, and she hanged herself. And I thought, this may be the best thing to do. How-- how can you live with all those images, those things? It's not what they did to you, but you saw-- the things that you saw that is unbelievable. So I said to [INAUDIBLE], and I was really in a mess. So slowly, the doctor was talking day and night to me. And he said, 'Listen, [? you'll ?] get your husband back.' And the doctor told me I could not have children anymore, that I was burned inside, and it was no good. And that was no good. He says, 'Why don't you adopt a child?' So I came back. My husband was not very-- he was not very keen on children. He was a painter. He wanted to be free. He did not want to be tied down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and we talked about it, and talked about it. And then I-- I-- suddenly, I-- I started to bleed very bad. I had to go to the doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the doctor said, 'You had a miscarriage.' I said, 'What, a miscarriage? I'm not supposed to get children.'

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, 'yeah, there is still some-- a little part of the--' And I was-- [SOBBING] all the time said, 'I want to have a child. I want to have a child. I want to have a little girl like my-- my mother, [INAUDIBLE].' I missed my mother so much. My father too, but you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- and I got pregnant. And that's not an easy pregnant-- pregnant. And I got Marty. And I was one of the very, very few. And all the people who were in Block 10 couldn't get children anymore. That was a blessing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Maybe that I had a couple operations that I missed. And the doctors himself said he did not understand why I could carry it out. I did. So I get Marty. And then  my husband took off. He could-- didn't want the responsibility. He went off, and he went in the mental hospital. He was in the mental hospital. He is not very well. I get-- actually, you know, I have a picture of him here. I can show you later.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and then slowly, I-- I had to go on, and I think he died. I don't know what happened to him. And then he went away with somebody else. So I was alone with the child. So I was-- and then I had to-- I had to go on while I had the child. Did you-- we have to move this along. Did you-- When did you come to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Canada in 1954. Martin was born in 1947, just after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived in Holland until then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I lived in-- after that, I lived in Amsterdam, and then I came-- my brother was here. My brother came here. His friend was here who had helped him also in Auschwitz. [? Is a ?] story by himself. And then they have-- I then came here. And I start to work as a designer here. And then I got married in 19-- and that man died, Feldman. And then after, I met Irwin. Now, Irwin-- he was family from my second husband. And [INAUDIBLE] parents were a little bit related. And he-- and he was always traveling, and he put his suitcases downstairs in the-- in the house. So he was-- I came in. I knew him, but I never thought I would--

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was the one who slowly-- then my brother died, my sister died, my sister-in-law died. I didn't-- in three months, a sister-- in four months' time, I was-- I was, again, in such a mess. I had nothing left in four months' time. So-- and Irwin was the one who always took me. And I was crying, and I didn't want to leave, didn't want to, you know. So he said, 'Why don't you to tell me something?' And he was-- he was the one who-- he was always [INAUDIBLE] take you, don't leave you if you would go with me. Slowly, we started to-- to-- he was much younger than I. Have a life together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Have a life together.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand that you do-- you go to schools, and you talk about your Holocaust experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It took me a long time. That's only five years. I never could talk about it-- absolutely not. But slowly, after the Anne Frank exhibition, I started to talk a bit. Now, I go once a month. I go to the School of the Deaf a couple times.

speaker: interviewer
text: You volunteer there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I feel very fond of those children. It's something from-- from youth. I got just a very beautiful letter from the deaf school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still doing any designing or any kind of dressmaking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I do designing, but then I don't want-- a little sewing. Then I gave it to my girlfriend to sew. I cut her dresses, you know, make some patterns-- not too bad, you know. Lately, I'm very lazy. I got also very old. But then I said, you know, it's-- life is-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any kind of special ideas or thoughts that you'd like to leave with us about how you feel about life, how it affected you, and something to leave for the future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing is, all the kids always ask me-- really, they always ask me, 'Do you hate the Germans, or do you--' you know? And I always said, 'No, I don't hate them. I just can't understand.'

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't mentally not understand how you can do-- do it. I am in wonder it, how people can-- can be so hateful. OK, there is big hate. But to hate to kill?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- it's nothing-- can't you live with each other without-- I always say that it's-- I can't understand that people can be that unbelievable. And, you know, when you come back, actually, you never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still haunted by nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I still do, sure. And-- you're always in the camp. You can't get out, you know. It's-- it's so long ago. And, you know, your mind is such a crazy thing. Your body can heal everything. Your mind is absolutely-- you can't say, 'I don't want to think about it.' You can't. It just comes back. And out of the-- out of the blue air, you know, you suddenly see again there's something which you see everything. Oh, god. There it is again. And you go, and there lay all those people again. It's unbelievable. You can't get it out of your mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: But talking about it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Talking about it helps, and I still go to a psychiatrist. Still, after all those years. Not so much anymore. I go once a month, I go once or twice. I just talk about it, and I have very big help in [? Irv. ?] I can talk about it, you know. And it's-- [SOBBING] sometimes, you think life is supposed to be normal, but it isn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: You get to see your son a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I see him. Not too often, but I see him, yeah. I see him with the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grandchildren have you got?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two. Those two kids. Those two kids, and one is 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does your son live?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my son lives in Ottawa. He is-- he is a doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does he know that you're doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He doesn't-- he-- he can't understand he-- I-- I never told him. It's only the couple last years that he said, 'You never told me anything. You never told--'

speaker: interviewer
text: When are you going to tell your little son what you went through? You don't. You want them to be happy and normal. You're not going to say-- But he said he knew it. [INAUDIBLE] I answered, 'But you never asked,' you know? Maybe I would have told him [INAUDIBLE] something. So yeah, we were talking. 'I saw the number on your arm.' And I was also afraid to open up wounds, you know? It goes from both sides. Do you ever talk about it with friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: The funny part is when you come, just, sorry-- you come just to-- of-- a lot of people in Canada-- they say it's like, 'Yeah, we went through a lot, too.' Nobody understands really [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what it meant. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Not what they do to you, so many things. And you think, God in heaven, why? And you just say, Why? Why in heaven's sake, why did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They [? met ?] in a play from the-- from the deaf organization. And they played the prodigal son. And my uncle [INAUDIBLE], and my mother plays the-- the son. And he asks forgiveness. And they both are in it. And that is made 1938, before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? That was in Amsterdam. That was in Amsterdam. We made it in the place where we had the community, like the whole-- it was like a group of people who came always together. And they played-- made plays, and did all kinds of things. That is me, that is about-- I was about 12 years old, 10 or 12. And I always didn't like the picture where I was-- I had-- I had bad ankles, and I always had to wear high shoes-- high laced shoes, high-laced shoes. And I was already a little bit a young girl, and I hated that I had to wear them. I must be 17-- 16, 17-- 60 years ago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was just a nice picture. [LAUGHS] I don't know what to say. It was-- the-- actually, the nephew like [? Teo ?] [INAUDIBLE] a sister from the-- from [INAUDIBLE]. He had a son. He was also photo-- he made like art pictures. And he made it. [? Teo's ?] son, and I forget his name. And then we were both on it. We were very young. Yeah. That is a picture that was made when we were a member [INAUDIBLE] the [INAUDIBLE]. It was the [? Arbeiders ?] Jeugd Centrale-- that means, like, the Worker's Youth Center. And-- and [INAUDIBLE] was also in-- in part was in sports. And we called it Workers Sports Group. Now, we were both swimming. We were both in-- life savers, like in special. And my husband stays in it-- [INAUDIBLE] husband and me. But I think I'm the one- the fat one with the cap on. And I-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year? That was-- that was before the war. That must be 1935, 1937, around there. I did this picture with my first husband, Max. [INAUDIBLE] and that's me. My hair is-- I made it lighter. I did not want it to be so very dark, so visible. And that was in 1941. It was before we were taken away, before we went in hiding. And it was-- and we wear already the stars. The stars were-- well, end of 1940, 1941, we got the stars. We both have stars on. And that is-- that is my older sister, Sophia. And she did not want to go in hiding before all her people had place to go where-- had place to go. And in the end of everything, she was picked up with her baby, and didn't come back. And her-- her two sisters and her brother came back, but not her. She did not want to go in hiding before we all were safe. That's Sophia-- she went with her baby. It's Lillian and my brother Joe. Now, Lillian was all the time in Auschwitz when we-- we were picked up together in 1943. And we came both through they war. And if it wouldn't be for her, or it wouldn't be for me, we wouldn't both be not here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and my brother, who was also in Auschwitz. And when he was very hurt, they took him to Russia. And they brought him back. That was made in-- yeah, in 1943. What was [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: February--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. That was-- we were in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- we were-- we would ask-- the doctor had asked what they actually put into me. And we didn't ever know. So Irwin asked one of the consulate if they could find out what happened. And they found only two things that they knew about. And so that came out in Nuremberg trial that Professor-- Professor [? Klauberg ?] knew that that was used, but there was far more used, and they don't know nothing about it. The only two ones what they know about it, they gave us the-- they admit that they used [INAUDIBLE]. And the rest was all-- was not-- could not give us the [INAUDIBLE] for it. They know there was far more things used, but they-- they just the two that they knew. That-- that document is that I got from Germany. That-- that is an apology for the experiments they did on me. And they gave me 3,000 [? Reichsmark. ?] Don't forget, they took everything what I owned-- everything, the house and everything. And when I came back, I-- I had only old clothes on. They gave me 3,000 mark to buy some clothes and to make it a little bit easier. And they apologized for the things they did to me. That is Martin. That is my son. And he was born in 1954-- aah, 1947. And it was just after the war, and and a very, very hard time. I was not supposed to have kids, but I got one. I could never after, [? it never , ?] but I got one child.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very, very lucky. 90% of the people who were in Block 10-- 95%-- couldn't get children anymore. Wasn't mine a miracle? He is married, and he has two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is my-- my two grandchildren-- one is [? Matthew. ?] He is the oldest. And [? Benjamin ?] is the youngest. And the right one is the oldest. And they do very well. I love them dearly. One is in figure skating, and one plays piano, and do other things. I'm very proud of them. And they sweet. And that is-- is Irwin. And he is-- he is the one who helped me to talk about it. It was at least five years ago that I absolutely could not say a word about it. I was crying in the night, just [INAUDIBLE]. But I ever-- and he asked me many times, 'Tell me something, tell me,' and I could not. But he was the one who slowly started. He said, 'If you don't talk about it, and there are so many people say it didn't happen. And you should say.' And all the people before-- before they die, used to say, 'Don't forget me.' That's the last word I heard from everybody who died. And then he said, 'If you want people to not forget the people, then you better talk about it.'

speaker: interviewee
text: So slowly, but surely, he kept me going. And it was the first time I talked about Anne Frank exhibition. And-- and he volunteers for me all the time. He tells me where-- that I should do that or not do it . Otherwise, I still would say, 'I can't do it, I can't do it.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you've got to realize that a lot of people in this situation cannot talk about it. They keep it locked up. I'm fortunate. My wife is strong enough to talk about it. And I think not only does it help the people she talks to, but in some ways, it does help her. It's like talking to a psychiatrist. And I hope more people-- if they opened up, they would not be so tense. And I thank the Lord that she is so strong and pretty. [LAUGHING]
